role_hints.oews_basis: 23-2011 Court Reporters and Simultaneous Captioners — NOT in the record's listed top-10 because role_mix is a 4-digit 5614 proxy dominated by contact-center occupations; role_mix is effectively unrepresentative for this code — hypothesis: reporters plus freelance equivalents are the dominant production cost, and many are 1099 freelancers outside the W-2 wage bill entirely
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: est. 30-50% of W-2 wages, more of true production cost including 1099 payouts — flagged as hypothesis
role_hints.note: AI speech recognition now produces a rough verbatim draft at near-zero cost; what it cannot yet do is swear witnesses, certify the record, or carry the officer-of-the-court role. The paid human element is compressing toward capture-plus-certification; realtime stenographic skill still commands a premium where rules require it.

role_hints.oews_basis: 31-9094 Medical Transcriptionists 1.25% appears in the 4-digit proxy; general transcription/scoping sits in the tail and in 1099 freelance networks
role_hints.note: Cleaning ASR drafts into certified transcripts is exactly what current AI does well; human review shrinks to a QA pass.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 25.9% in the proxy overstates this via contact-center contamination; at 561492 these are calendar coordinators, production and exhibit staff
role_hints.note: Job scheduling, reporter assignment, transcript production tracking and delivery are routine coordination — near-fully automatable.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 7.5% + 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 4.0% (proxy shares)
role_hints.note: Law-firm relationship management and litigation-support selling persist; internal ops reporting automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: within 23-2011 (captioners) — not separately listed; this code also houses realtime captioning services
role_hints.note: ADA-driven live captioning demand is growing and quality-sensitive; ASR captioning is already substituting at the low end (education, webinars) while legal/broadcast realtime holds longer.

source_hints.source: National Court Reporters Association (NCRA) — workforce and industry data
source_hints.why: The stenographic trade association; shortage statistics, certification counts, and its advocacy materials reveal where ASR/digital reporting is displacing stenographers.

source_hints.source: AAERT (American Association of Electronic Reporters and Transcribers)
source_hints.why: The digital-reporting association — its certification growth is direct evidence of the product-replacement channel (digital capture + transcription vs live steno).

source_hints.source: Ducker Worldwide Court Reporting Industry Outlook (the 'Ducker Report', 2013-14, NCRA-commissioned)
source_hints.why: The standard source for reporter age structure and the projected shortage — dated; verify whether NCRA has published updated workforce numbers before relying on 2014 figures.

source_hints.source: Veritext and US Legal Support acquisition announcements and PE ownership history
source_hints.why: The two dominant PE-backed consolidators; their deal cadence and any disclosed terms are the best evidence for active_consolidators and buy-side appetite in a tiny industry.

source_hints.source: State statutory transcript page-rate schedules and federal court reporter fee schedules (Judicial Conference maximum transcript rates)
source_hints.why: Primary documents for the per-page pricing mechanism — statutory rates cap official-work pricing and anchor freelance depo rates.

source_hints.source: Speech-to-Text Institute (STTI) publications on the reporter shortage and digital transition
source_hints.why: Vendor-side coalition material quantifying the shortage-driven shift to digital; the organization may have wound down — confirm it still exists before citing.

capture_questions: Transcripts are priced per page (statutory schedules for official/court work, market rates plus appearance fees for freelance deposition work) — when AI transcription collapses production cost per page, do agencies keep the spread as margin, or do law-firm procurement and digital-first competitors (already marketing lower page rates) bid the page rate down toward the new cost floor?

Who owns the client: the agency holds the law firm's calendar and preferred-vendor status, but the certified reporter is the officer who appears — freelance reporters routinely take relationships with them. In an acquisition, what book actually transfers, and what does reporter (not client) churn do to it?

What stops a litigation client from bypassing agencies entirely — remote deposition platforms with built-in ASR plus an AAERT-certified digital reporter, or in some jurisdictions a notarized recording — and which state court rules still make that impossible vs merely unconventional?

Which states still require a licensed stenographic CSR for depositions vs permit digital/voice-writer reporting, and how fast are those rules moving given the documented reporter shortage — is the regulatory moat widening or draining?
terminal_value_question: The paid product here is a certified verbatim record of a legal proceeding. Mature ASR produces the verbatim text at near-zero marginal cost; what remains purchasable is the oath, the certification, the chain-of-custody liability, and realtime delivery where rules demand it. Do state CSR statutes and court rules keep a paid human certifier in the loop — shrinking the product to a thin certification wrapper around AI transcription — or do rule changes (accelerated by the stenographer shortage itself) let certified-AI/digital workflows replace the product outright? Answer by naming the actual rule mechanism per major jurisdiction, and distinguish agencies cutting their transcription costs (margin story) from clients no longer needing what agencies sell (melt story).
special_notes: The record's role_mix (4-digit 5614) is dominated by contact-center occupations and omits court reporters entirely — treat it as unrepresentative; build V from the hypothesis structure above and say so in the output.

Freelance economics: a large share of deposition reporters are 1099 freelancers paid per page/appearance through agencies — the 0.2728 labor_share captures only W-2 staff, and agency P&Ls are intermediary spreads on freelance production; automating production changes the freelancer's income before the agency's.
Statutory page rates on official/court work cap upside there; the commercial deposition market is where pricing (and deflation) is set.

Tiny industry: n_total 3,004 firms, $3.1B receipts, n_band only 60 (51 in the 20-99 class, 9 in 100-499) — a single Veritext/USLS acquisition materially changes the universe; single-year data is noisy.
Captioning/CART (ADA-driven) demand inside this code is growing while deposition steno shrinks — the two sub-segments have opposite trajectories; identify the mix before scoring one number.
Consolidation by Veritext and US Legal Support is well documented — usable for B and M only, never as evidence of automatability or adoption (rule 2).
